title: Enteritis in young rabbit kits
subtitle: Gut disease in kits from weaning to twelve weeks, recognition and management.
excerpt: Enteritis, inflammation and infection of the gut, is the leading cause of death in rabbit kits from three to twelve weeks of age. In a colony system, it can move through a group quickly. Knowing the signs, removing affected animals promptly, and adjusting the diet saves kits and limits spread.
sourceNotes: University of Wisconsin Extension, Rabbit Production and Diseases. British Rabbit Council health and welfare guidance. APHA, Rabbit Enteric Conditions factsheet.

glossaryTerms.definition: Inflammation of the intestinal lining in rabbits, usually caused by bacterial infection (Clostridium spiroforme or E. coli) or diet-related gut disruption; characterised by diarrhoea, bloating, or sudden death.
Soft, mucus-coated droppings produced in the caecum and consumed directly from the anus by the rabbit; an essential part of normal rabbit nutrition and gut function.

content.text: Young rabbit kits have an immature gut microbiome for the first few weeks after weaning. During this window, an imbalance, caused by too many pellets too early, too little hay, stress, or bacterial challenge, can trigger
. The gut ferments abnormally, producing gas and bacterial toxins. In acute cases the kit dies within hours of first showing signs.
Watery or soft droppings, or droppings stuck around the vent area
Bloated abdomen, gurgling on gentle palpation
Hunched posture, reluctance to move, grinding teeth
Sudden death in a kit that was eating normally the previous day, often the first sign in a colony-level outbreak
Remove the affected kit from the colony pen immediately and isolate it in a clean, dry, warm space.
Offer unlimited hay. Remove all pellets until the droppings return to normal.
Provide oral rehydration fluid (electrolyte solution for small animals) in the water for the first 24 hours.
If more than one kit in the colony shows signs within 24 hours, call your vet, an antibiotic course across the group may be warranted to limit spread.
Two things reduce the risk: don't wean before six weeks, and make sure hay is available from the moment kits start eating alongside the doe. Kits raised with constant hay access have a more settled
habit by weaning and a more robust gut to face the change. Don't switch pellet brands at weaning: any diet change stresses a gut that is already under pressure.
